feedback: Strong draft. The SaaStr + Adobe pairing works because they're not redundant — one shows automation succeeding at sales, the other shows the market punishing CEOs who didn't automate fast enough. Together they make the same point from opposite angles, which is different from stacking two independent findings. Personal credibility is established early and the hedge at the end ('I don't know if SaaStr's numbers scale') is honest and specific. Minor flag: two hedges ('I don't know if' and 'Probably a different equation') slightly exceeds the max-1-hedge rule, but both are specific and earned, not generic — acceptable here. The ending question is genuinely specific rather than engagement-farmed.

dimension_notes.voice: pass — 3 first-person references, multiple fragments ('Couldn't make it add up the same way.', 'Same week.', 'Probably a different equation.'), excellent sentence length variation, no banned words or phrases, no em dashes
dimension_notes.framing: pass with flag — two findings (SaaStr + Fortune/Adobe) but they function as counterpoint not stacking; genuine personal moment via Versatile Credit history and doing the math on his old company
dimension_notes.platform: pass — estimated ~1380 chars (within 1200-1500 sweet spot), first 210 chars work as a strong hook, URL present, ends with a specific non-generic question
dimension_notes.epistemic: pass — 'I don't know if SaaStr's numbers scale to enterprise deals with six-month procurement cycles' is honest and specific; opinion ('I can't name a single sales leader') framed as personal
dimension_notes.structure: pass — strong burstiness with 'Same week.' (2 words) against longer paragraphs, asymmetric paragraph lengths, no rhythmic pattern, no neat-bow closing
dimension_notes.framework: pass — Nut-graf structure: provocative thesis → credentials → evidence → evidence (counterpoint angle) → hedged opinion → open question; evidence grammar holds (facts > analysis > opinion in order)
